metadata.title: Advanced Game Design: A Systems Approach — Book Review
metadata.color_semantics: claude fidelity: INK #3D3929; SPARK #D97757 one accent.
metadata.author: Seth Brown
tags: Advanced Game Design: A Systems Approach
metadata.note: HAI Fellows batch v2 — Teardown register, @NikBearBrown, Remotion animated bookends. Credit: Seth Brown — Humanitarians AI Fellow. HONESTY: all numbers trace to source article.

beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. Systemic design theory grounds ad hoc practice in transferable, teachable principles. Progression is modeled by curve families: linear, polynomial, exponential, and logistic. The probability section is mathematically correct and accessible to non-specialists. A key tension is that dopamine-loop design and elegant design are not the same goal.

artifactLines: Systemic design theory grounds ad hoc practice in transferable, teachable principles.
Progression is modeled by curve families: linear, polynomial, exponential, and logistic.
The probability section is mathematically correct and accessible to non-specialists.
A key tension is that dopamine-loop design and elegant design are not the same goal.

beats.narration_text: The power-curve families, linear through logistic, plus the tension that dopamine-loop design and elegant design are not the same, made me want to plot real progression. Help me: (1) sketch how a player's power grows under a linear versus an exponential curve, (2) explain when a logistic S-curve better models late-game plateauing, and (3) name one mechanic where a dopamine loop and elegant design pull apart, and say which curve each would favor.

beats.narration_text: Methodological Soundness: The probability section is mathematically correct and accessible. Progression and power curves: linear (y=Ax+B), polynomial (y=x^n), exponential (y=A^x), logistic/sigmoid (S-curve), piecewise linear, NAP (near-arithmetic progression)

beats.narration_text: Tension 2: The elegance ideal versus commercial reality. Dopamine-loop design and elegant design are not the same thing, and the book provides no analysis of when they conflict.
